verses.text: In the three and twentieth year of Joash the son of Ahaziah king of Judah Jehoahaz the son of Jehu began to reign over Israel in Samaria, and reigned seventeen years. And he did that which was evil in the sight of the LORD, and followed the sins of Jeroboam the son of Nebat, which made Israel to sin; he departed not therefrom. And the anger of the LORD was kindled against Israel, and he delivered them into the hand of Hazael king of Syria, and into the hand of Benhadad the son of Hazael, all their days. Guidance: Jehoahaz of Israel and the Aramean scourge. The narrative returns north to Jehu's son Jehoahaz, who reigns 17 years in Samaria. The verdict is the now-familiar northern indictment: he 'did that which was evil... followed the sins of Jeroboam... departed not therefrom.' The four-generation Jehu dynasty (10:30) continues, but so does the calf-worship that doomed the northern kingdom from its founding. The consequence is severe: 'the anger of the LORD was kindled against Israel, and he delivered them into the hand of Hazael king of Syria, and into the hand of Benhadad the son of Hazael, all their days.' Aram — under Hazael and then his son Ben-hadad III — oppresses Israel throughout Jehoahaz's reign. The chapter is showing us the LORD's discipline operating exactly as Deuteronomy warned (Deut 28): persistent covenant-breaking brings foreign oppression. The instrument is Hazael, whom Elisha foresaw with tears (8:12). The chapter is teaching us that the LORD's anger is not arbitrary temper but covenantal response — 'kindled against Israel' because Israel persisted in the foundational sin it would not abandon. The chapter is also setting up the surprising movement of grace that follows: even in His anger, the LORD will hear a cry from this evil king. The discipline is real, but it is corrective, aimed at a turning, not at annihilation.

Aram — under Hazael and then his son Ben-hadad III — oppresses Israel throughout Jehoahaz's reign. The chapter is showing us the LORD's discipline operating exactly as Deuteronomy warned (Deut 28): persistent covenant-breaking brings foreign oppression. And Jehoahaz besought the LORD, and the LORD hearkened unto him: for he saw the oppression of Israel, because the king of Syria oppressed them. And the LORD gave Israel a saviour, so that they went out from under the hand of the Syrians: and the children of Israel dwelt in their tents, as beforetime. Nevertheless they departed not from the sins of the house of Jeroboam, who made Israel sin, but walked therein: and there remained the grove also in Samaria. Guidance: A cry heard, a saviour given — and yet no repentance. In his oppression Jehoahaz 'besought the LORD' (Hebrew chillah — entreated the favor of), and astonishingly 'the LORD hearkened unto him: for he saw the oppression of Israel.' The evil king cries out, and the LORD — moved by the suffering of His people — answers. He 'gave Israel a saviour' (probably Jehoash and Jeroboam II, whose victories would roll back Aram, or possibly the rise of Assyria under Adad-nirari III which pressed Aram from the north and relieved Israel), so that Israel escaped the Aramean grip and 'dwelt in their tents, as beforetime' — a return to peaceful normalcy. But the relief produced no lasting change of heart: 'Nevertheless they departed not from the sins of the house of Jeroboam... and there remained the grove also in Samaria.' The Asherah-pole still stood. The chapter is showing us one of Scripture's most striking displays of divine mercy: the LORD hears even an unrepentant, evil king's cry in distress, because He sees His people's affliction and is moved by it. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD's compassion is not earned by our worthiness but flows from His own pity — He answers the cry of suffering even when the crier has not reformed. And yet the chapter is also teaching the tragedy of mercy received without repentance: Israel takes the deliverance and keeps the sin. The grove remains in Samaria. The chapter is showing us that rescue from consequences does not, by itself, change hearts — relief can be received gratefully in the moment and then squandered as the people return to the very sins that brought the oppression.

Neither did he leave of the people to Jehoahaz but fifty horsemen, and ten chariots, and ten thousand footmen; for the king of Syria had destroyed them, and had made them like the dust by threshing. Guidance: Israel ground to dust. The narrator measures how far Aram had reduced Israel's military: Jehoahaz was left with only 'fifty horsemen, and ten chariots, and ten thousand footmen.' For a kingdom that under Ahab had fielded thousands of chariots, this is a skeletal remnant — token cavalry, almost no chariotry, a small infantry. The cause: 'the king of Syria had destroyed them, and had made them like the dust by threshing.' The image is brutal: Israel had been threshed like grain on a threshing floor, beaten and crushed until what remained was chaff-dust. (The phrase recalls Amos 1:3, which condemns Hazael for having 'threshed Gilead with threshing instruments of iron.') The chapter is showing us the depth of Israel's humiliation under the LORD's discipline — the proud northern military machine reduced to dust. The chapter is teaching us the concrete cost of persistent sin: it is not an abstract spiritual deficit but a kingdom stripped of its defenses, a people pulverized by an enemy the LORD permitted to chastise them. The chapter is also heightening the contrast with the mercy of v4-5: the LORD gave a saviour and relief NOT because Israel had recovered strength (they had almost none) but purely out of compassion for their oppression. The fifty horsemen could not have saved themselves; the deliverance, when it came, was unmistakably the LORD's gift, not Israel's achievement. The dust-remnant makes the grace stand out all the more starkly.

Now the rest of the acts of Jehoahaz, and all that he did, and his might, are they not written in the book of the chronicles of the kings of Israel? And Jehoahaz slept with his fathers; and they buried him in Samaria: and Joash his son reigned in his stead. Guidance: Jehoahaz's regnal close. The standard formula closes the reign: the annal-reference, death, burial in Samaria, and succession by his son Joash (Jehoash of Israel — not to be confused with the contemporaneous Joash of Judah of the previous chapter; the two kingdoms had kings of the same name at the same time, a recurring source of reader-confusion the narrator simply lives with). The chapter is closing the account of a king who was evil yet whose desperate cry the LORD heard. Jehoahaz never reformed — he kept the calves and the grove — but in his affliction he turned, however imperfectly, toward the LORD, and the LORD answered for the people's sake. The chapter is teaching us, in this quiet death-formula, that even a flawed and incomplete turning toward God in distress is not despised by Him. The deliverance Jehoahaz's cry secured would bear fruit in his son and grandson's military recoveries (v25; 14:25). The chapter is also continuing the steady march of the Jehu dynasty toward its four-generation limit — Jehu, Jehoahaz, now Joash, with only Jeroboam II and the brief Zechariah to come. The clock the LORD set in 10:30 keeps ticking even as the kingdom rises and falls under Aramean pressure.

In the thirty and seventh year of Joash king of Judah began Jehoash the son of Jehoahaz to reign over Israel in Samaria, and reigned sixteen years. And he did that which was evil in the sight of the LORD; he departed not from all the sins of Jeroboam the son of Nebat, who made Israel sin: but he walked therein. And the rest of the acts of Joash, and all that he did, and his might wherewith he fought against Amaziah king of Judah, are they not written in the book of the chronicles of the kings of Israel? And Joash slept with his fathers; and Jeroboam sat upon his throne: and Joash was buried in Samaria with the kings of Israel. Guidance: Jehoash of Israel introduced and summarized. Jehu's grandson Jehoash (Joash of Israel) reigns 16 years. The verdict is the same northern formula — evil, walked in Jeroboam's sins. The narrator then unusually gives his regnal-closing summary up front (v12-13), noting 'his might wherewith he fought against Amaziah king of Judah' (the war recorded in 14:8-14, where Jehoash defeats and humiliates the southern king) and his burial in Samaria, with his son Jeroboam (Jeroboam II) succeeding. The chapter is showing us another king in the Jehu line who continues the calf-worship while presiding over a military recovery. The narrator's placement of the closing-summary here, before the Elisha death-scene that follows, signals that the Elisha episode (v14-19) belongs chronologically within Jehoash's reign and is theologically pivotal to understanding his military successes. The chapter is teaching us, by this evaluation, the persistent pattern of the north: even kings whom the LORD allows to win battles and recover territory remain under the standing indictment of the calf-cult. Military 'might' is recorded, but it never earns the verdict 'did right' — because the heart-loyalty to the LORD is still withheld. The chapter is preparing us for the deathbed scene in which this evil king will weep over the dying Elisha and receive a prophecy of the very victories the summary has just mentioned.

Now Elisha was fallen sick of his sickness whereof he died. And Joash the king of Israel came down unto him, and wept over his face, and said, O my father, my father, the chariot of Israel, and the horsemen thereof. Guidance: The dying prophet and the weeping king. After roughly sixty years of ministry, Elisha falls into his final illness. The reigning king, Jehoash of Israel — an evil king by the chapter's own verdict — comes down to the prophet's deathbed and weeps over him, crying, 'O my father, my father, the chariot of Israel, and the horsemen thereof.' These are the exact words Elisha himself had cried over the departing Elijah (2 Kgs 2:12). The chapter is showing us a remarkable moment. An idolatrous king, who walked in Jeroboam's sins all his days, nonetheless recognizes that the dying prophet is Israel's true defense — its real 'chariots and horsemen,' worth more than the fifty horsemen and ten chariots Aram had left the nation (v7). Jehoash weeps not from personal piety but from a genuine, if inconsistent, grasp of the truth: the prophet's presence had been Israel's actual security. The chapter is teaching us that even the spiritually compromised can perceive, in a moment of loss, where true strength lies. The chapter is also marking the closing of the great prophetic era. Elijah was taken in fire; Elisha dies of ordinary sickness in his bed — a quieter end, but the same title is spoken over him. The man through whom the LORD parted the Jordan, raised the dead, fed the hungry, healed Naaman, and blinded armies is dying. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD's servants are mortal; the power was never in Elisha but in the LORD who worked through him — and even now, on his deathbed, that power has not finished its work, as the next verses will show.

And Elisha said unto him, Take bow and arrows. And he took unto him bow and arrows. And he said to the king of Israel, Put thine hand upon the bow. And he put his hand upon it: and Elisha put his hands upon the king's hands. And he said, Open the window eastward. And he opened it. Then Elisha said, Shoot. And he shot. And he said, The arrow of the LORD's deliverance, and the arrow of deliverance from Syria: for thou shalt smite the Syrians in Aphek, till thou have consumed them. Guidance: The arrow of the LORD's deliverance. Even dying, Elisha performs one last prophetic sign-act. He has the king take a bow and arrows, put his hand on the bow, and then lays his own dying hands over the king's hands — a vivid image of prophetic power channeling divine promise through the king's action. He has the eastward window opened (east toward the Transjordan territory Aram had seized) and commands, 'Shoot.' As the arrow flies, Elisha declares it: 'The arrow of the LORD's deliverance, and the arrow of deliverance from Syria: for thou shalt smite the Syrians in Aphek, till thou have consumed them.' The shot is a prophetic guarantee of victory over Aram at Aphek (the site of an earlier Israelite victory, 1 Kgs 20:26-30). The chapter is showing us the LORD's deliverance secured not by the king's strength but by the prophet's hand laid over his — the victory is the LORD's gift, mediated through His prophet even as that prophet lies dying. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD's promises come with His own initiative: Elisha puts his hands on the king's hands; the deliverance originates in the LORD's power, not the king's archery. The chapter is also showing us the LORD's continued faithfulness to undeserving Israel — He promises this evil king victory over the oppressor, fulfilling the mercy begun when Jehoahaz cried out (v4). The dying prophet's last gift to a faithless king is a word of triumph for the LORD's afflicted people. But the next verse will reveal that the SCOPE of the victory depends on something the king himself must supply.

And he said, Take the arrows. And he took them. And he said unto the king of Israel, Smite upon the ground. And he smote thrice, and stayed. And the man of God was wroth with him, and said, Thou shouldest have smitten five or six times; then hadst thou smitten Syria till thou hadst consumed it: whereas now thou shalt smite Syria but thrice. Guidance: The arrows on the ground — and a half-hearted test. Elisha gives the king a second sign. 'Take the arrows... Smite upon the ground.' The king strikes the ground with the arrows three times and stops. Elisha is angry: 'Thou shouldest have smitten five or six times; then hadst thou smitten Syria till thou hadst consumed it: whereas now thou shalt smite Syria but thrice.' The number of strikes determined the number of victories — and Jehoash, lacking zeal and faith, stopped at three. The chapter is showing us a sobering principle about how we receive the LORD's promises. The first sign (the shot arrow) guaranteed deliverance; this second sign measured its EXTENT, and the extent was set by the king's own response. Jehoash struck listlessly — perhaps puzzled, perhaps half-believing, perhaps simply without passion for the complete defeat of his enemy — and so received only a partial victory. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD's deliverances are often given in proportion to the faith and earnestness with which we lay hold of them. The promise was generous ('till thou have consumed them'); the king's small response capped what he would actually receive. The chapter is teaching us not to be content with a few half-hearted strikes when God has invited us to ask and act for total victory. Elisha's anger is the anger of a man who sees a king settling for a fraction of what the LORD was willing to give. The chapter is showing us that unbelief and lukewarmness do not forfeit God's mercy entirely (the king still gets three victories) but they do diminish it — we often have as much of God's promised help as we have the faith to pursue.

And Elisha died, and they buried him. And the bands of the Moabites invaded the land at the coming in of the year. And it came to pass, as they were burying a man, that, behold, they spied a band of men; and they cast the man into the sepulchre of Elisha: and when the man was let down, and touched the bones of Elisha, he revived, and stood up on his feet. Guidance: Elisha dies — and his bones raise the dead. The great prophet dies and is buried. Then the narrator records one final, astonishing sign. Moabite raiding-bands were invading the land seasonally. During one such raid, some Israelites burying a man spotted the raiders and, in haste, threw the corpse into Elisha's tomb. 'When the man was let down, and touched the bones of Elisha, he revived, and stood up on his feet.' Contact with the dead prophet's bones raised a dead man to life. The chapter is closing Elisha's ministry with a miracle that happens AFTER his death — a posthumous resurrection, the only one of its kind in Scripture. The chapter is teaching us a profound truth: the resurrection power that operated through Elisha's life was never his own; it was the LORD's, and it lingered in the very bones of His faithful servant. Elisha had raised the Shunammite's son by stretching his living body over the boy (4:34); now his dead bones raise a stranger. The chapter is showing us that the LORD's power is not extinguished by the death of His servant — death could not stop the life-giving work the LORD chose to associate with this prophet. The chapter is also offering a quiet foreshadowing of resurrection hope: a dead man touches the remains of God's prophet and rises. It points forward to the truth that in the LORD there is life that death cannot hold. The chapter is teaching us that faithful service to God has effects that outlast the servant's own lifetime — Elisha's last recorded act is to give life from beyond the grave.

But Hazael king of Syria oppressed Israel all the days of Jehoahaz. And the LORD was gracious unto them, and had compassion on them, and had respect unto them, because of his covenant with Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, and would not destroy them, neither cast he them from his presence as yet. Guidance: The deep reason for the mercy: the covenant with the fathers. The narrator pauses to give the theological foundation of all the mercy shown in this chapter. Hazael oppressed Israel throughout Jehoahaz's reign — the discipline was real and prolonged. 'And the LORD was gracious unto them, and had compassion on them, and had respect unto them.' Three words for mercy piled up — gracious, compassionate, regarding. And the REASON is named: 'because of his covenant with Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, and would not destroy them, neither cast he them from his presence as yet.' The chapter is showing us the bedrock beneath Israel's survival. Israel did not deserve to be spared — they kept the calves and the grove, their kings were evil, they did not repent. So why were they not destroyed? Because of the LORD's ancient covenant with the patriarchs. His faithfulness to promises made centuries earlier held back the destruction that Israel's sin deserved. The chapter is teaching us the most reassuring and humbling truth about the LORD's dealings with His people: their continued existence rests not on their merit but on His covenant fidelity. He remembers Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob when the present generation gives Him no reason to remember them. The phrase 'as yet' is sobering, though — it signals that this restraint is not unlimited; a day of casting-out will eventually come (the Assyrian exile of ch 17). The chapter is teaching us that covenant mercy is patient and long but not infinite; it gives Israel time, again and again, to return — and the tragedy is that Israel will keep refusing until the patience is finally exhausted.

So Hazael king of Syria died; and Benhadad his son reigned in his stead. And Jehoash the son of Jehoahaz took again out of the hand of Benhadad the son of Hazael the cities, which he had taken out of the hand of Jehoahaz his father by war. Three times did Joash beat him, and recovered the cities of Israel. Guidance: The prophecy fulfilled — exactly three times. Hazael, the scourge of Israel, finally dies; his son Ben-hadad III succeeds. And then Elisha's deathbed prophecy comes true with precise, almost painful accuracy: Jehoash recovers from Ben-hadad the cities Hazael had taken from his father Jehoahaz — and 'three times did Joash beat him, and recovered the cities of Israel.' Three victories. Exactly the number of times the king struck the ground with the arrows (v18-19), no more. The chapter is showing us the LORD's word fulfilled to the letter, including its limitation. Jehoash recovers the lost cities — the deliverance promised by the shot arrow is real — but only through three victories, because three was all the half-hearted king reached for. Had he struck five or six times, Aram would have been consumed; instead it is merely beaten back, and will trouble Israel again. The chapter is teaching us, in the fulfillment, the truth Elisha's anger had already declared: we receive as much of the LORD's promised deliverance as we lay hold of in faith. The cities come back, but the enemy survives — a partial victory that exactly matches a partial faith. The chapter is also closing the Elisha narrative on a note of vindicated prophecy: every word the dying prophet spoke proves true, both the promise and the cap. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD keeps His word with perfect precision — He gives all He promised, and the boundaries we set by our own unbelief are honored too. The book moves on, but the lesson stands: do not settle for three strikes when God offers total victory.
